turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Justice Thomas has the opinion of the Court this morning in case number 16-6219, Davila versus Davis.

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: This case comes to us on a writ of certiorari to the United States Court of Appeals for the Fifth Circuit. Federal habeas courts generally will not consider claims of error that a state court refused to hear because of inadequate and independent state procedural ground. This bar is known as Procedural Default. A state prisoner may be able to overcome the bar, however, if he can establish cause to excuse the procedural default and prejudice from the alleged error. If a prisoner's counsel provides ineffective assistance in state post-conviction proceedings, resulting in the procedural default of certain claims, that ineffective assistance generally will not qualify as cause to excuse the default. In Martinez v. Ryan, however, we announced a narrow equitable exception to this rule. The exception provides that a prisoner can establish cause to overcome a procedural default based on his post-conviction counsel's ineffective assistance when the defaulted claim is for ineffective assistance of trial counsel and where the state requires the prisoner to bring that claim in state post-conviction proceedings rather than on direct appeal after trial. The question presented in this case is whether we should extend that exception to allow federal courts to consider a different kind of defaulted claim, ineffective assistance of appellate counsel. In an opinion filed with the Clerk today, we hold that the exception in Martinez does not extend the claims of ineffective assistance of appellate counsel. Martinez does not purport to displace the general rule governing procedural default. And its underlying rationale does not support extending the exception to this context for three reasons. First, the Court in Martinez was uniquely concerned with the importance of the right tow effective counsel at trial, a right that it called a bedrock principle in our justice system. The special concern for effective assistance of trial counsel makes sense in light of the importance of the criminal trial relative to the appeal. Second, we do not need to need a special exception for claims of ineffective assistance of appellate counsel to ensure that meritorious claims of trial error receive review by at least one state or federal court. The vast majority of meritorious claims that would fall within the exception would either have been considered by trial court or would fall within the Martinez exception itself. Third, Martinez was responding to unique procedural considerations that do not apply to claims regarding appellate counsel. In light of these differences and in view of the significant cost and extension that Martinez would impose on the criminal justice system, we decline to extend Martinez. For these reasons and others set forth in our opinion, we affirm the judgment of the Fifth Circuit. Justice Breyer has filed a dissenting opinion in which Justices Ginsburg, Sotomayor, and Kagan have joined.
